title: Pierogi ruskie
subtitle: Polish potato and cheese dumplings, boiled then fried in butter with caramelised onion.
excerpt: Pierogi ruskie are the most popular pierogi in Poland: half-moon dumplings filled with mashed potato and farmer's cheese, boiled until tender and then tossed in a buttered pan with caramelised onion. The filling is mild and comforting; the browned onion and butter are the point. Takes time to make but is easy and scales well for a crowd.
sourceNotes: Pierogi ruskie are among the most widely eaten pierogi in Poland, despite the name (ruskie means 'Ruthenian' or from the Lwów / Lviv region rather than Russian). The potato and twaróg filling is documented throughout Polish culinary literature of the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Outside Poland, cottage cheese or quark is the closest widely available substitute for twaróg.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze uncooked pierogi on a baking tray until solid, then transfer to a bag. Cook from frozen in boiling salted water for five to six minutes. Do not defrost first.
recipe.batchNotes: Makes a large batch efficiently. The filling can be made a day ahead and refrigerated.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the filling a day ahead. Shape the pierogi the day they will be served, or freeze immediately after shaping.

recipeTools.notes: For boiling the pierogi. Needs enough water depth to let them float freely.
For frying the boiled pierogi in butter.

content.text: Pierogi are not quick. The dough is simple (flour, eggs, a little water) but it needs to be rolled thin and cut into rounds, each one filled and crimped shut. For thirty pierogi, expect to spend an hour on the shaping. The reward is that they freeze beautifully and cook from frozen in six minutes, which makes the investment worth it.

The filling for pierogi ruskie is potato and twaróg — the fresh, slightly sour farmer's cheese used throughout Polish cooking. Cottage cheese is the closest widely available substitute; drain it in a sieve for thirty minutes first to remove excess liquid. The ratio of potato to cheese should produce a filling that holds its shape but is not dry.

Boiling is the primary cooking method; the optional frying step that follows is what most Polish cooks consider essential. Boiled pierogi are pale and a little soft. Tossed in a hot buttered pan for two minutes, they pick up a golden colour and a slightly crisp edge that transforms the texture.

items.prepNote: drained in a sieve for 30 minutes
finely chopped, softened in butter until golden
thinly sliced, caramelised in butter until deep golden

content.text: Combine {{plain-flour}} of flour, the {{eggs}} egg, the melted butter, a pinch of salt, and 120 ml of warm water in a bowl. Mix until a shaggy dough forms, then knead on a floured surface for five minutes until smooth and elastic. Wrap in cling film and rest for thirty minutes.

Mash the cooked {{potato}} potatoes until smooth. Add the drained cottage cheese and the softened onion half. Season generously with salt and black pepper. Mix until combined. Taste — the filling should be well seasoned. Leave to cool.

Divide the dough into three pieces. On a lightly floured surface, roll one piece as thin as possible — 2 mm or less. Cut into circles using a 9 cm round cutter or the rim of a glass. Place a heaped teaspoon of filling in the centre of each round. Fold the dough over to form a half-moon and press the edges together firmly, then crimp with your fingers or the tines of a fork. Set aside on a floured tray. Repeat with the remaining dough and filling.

Bring a large pot of generously salted water to a rolling boil. Working in batches of ten, drop the pierogi in and cook for three to four minutes after they float to the surface. Remove with a slotted spoon and transfer to a buttered dish.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a wide frying pan over medium-high heat. Add the pierogi in a single layer and fry for two minutes on each side until golden. Meanwhile, caramelise the second {{onion}} sliced onion in a little butter in a separate pan over low heat for twenty minutes until very soft and deep golden. Serve the fried pierogi topped with caramelised onion and sour cream alongside.

items.symptom: The pierogi burst open while boiling
items.cause: The edges were not sealed firmly enough, or the filling was too wet.
items.fix: Press the edges firmly together and crimp tightly. Drain the cottage cheese for at least thirty minutes. Do not overfill.

items.symptom: The dough is tough and hard to roll thin
items.cause: Insufficient resting time, or the dough was worked too much.
items.fix: Rest the wrapped dough for a full thirty minutes. If it springs back while rolling, leave it for another ten minutes before continuing.

items.cause: Underseasoning — potato needs more salt than you think.
items.fix: Season the filling until it tastes slightly over-salted on its own; inside the dough it will taste correct.

content.text: Pierogi ruskie appear at Polish tables from Christmas Eve to everyday weeknight dinners. They are street food sold at outdoor markets, festival food, and the dish that Polish grandmothers are most consistently said to make best. The ruskie in the name refers not to Russia but to the historical Ruthenian region around Lwów (now Lviv, Ukraine), where this specific potato and cheese filling originated. The dish spread through Poland under this name and became the most popular variety, ahead of the meat-filled pierogi z mięsem and the sweet fruit-filled versions eaten as dessert.
